segments.s088-g9: تبیره is the noun heading the clause (a banner/standard in context). برآمد is a past tense intransitive verb meaning 'arose/issued forth' and follows the subject, a normal V position for poetic Persian. ز + درگاه شاه is a prepositional phrase using poetic ز (instead of modern از) to mark source/location: 'from the threshold/court of the king.'
segments.s088-g10: نهادند is a past‑tense transitive verb, 3rd person plural 'they placed/put.' The phrase بر سر is a fixed locative expression 'on the head,' followed by ز آهن as a possessive/partitive phrase 'of iron' (poetic ز for از), and کلاه is the direct object 'helmet/hat.' Word order is S V (implied they) + verb + locative + object.
segments.s088-g11: به is a locative preposition here ('in/at'), introducing the place phrase. پرده‌سرای is a compound/polite noun 'curtained-hall, pavilion' and is followed by افراسیاب (a proper name) in an attributive/genitive relationship; the intervening رد is part of the phrase and should be read as an epithet/marker attached to the place (poetic usage). Together the phrase indicates location: 'in Afrasiab's pavilion.'
segments.s088-g12: کسی را places the indefinite noun with the object/topic marker را; in negative constructions like this it yields the sense 'no one.' سر اندر نیامد is a negated past verb phrase literally 'the head did not come into' — an idiom meaning 'did not fall asleep' (نیامد = 'did not come', past). به خواب is the prepositional phrase 'into sleep/dream.'
segments.s088-g13: همه شب is a temporal adverbial 'all night' placed before the verb. همی is an archaic/epic particle indicating continuity or repeated action ('kept on / was continually'). لشکر is the subject 'the army/host' and آراستند is the past plural verb 'they arrayed/dressed/arranged.' The clause thus reads 'all night they kept arranging the army.'
segments.s088-g14: همی again marks continued/repeated action. تیغ و ژوپین are coordinated objects ('sword and gambeson' — two items of armament). بپیراستند is the past plural verb with the prefix ب- (here rendering the action 'they fitted/armed/arrayed' — a causative/inchoative sense in context). Word order is adverbial + objects + verb.
segments.s088-g15: This is a locational range: زمین کوه تا کوه uses two nouns joined by تا ('as far as / from ... to ...') — literally 'ground, mountain up to mountain' — followed by جوشن‌وران (the subject), 'the armored warriors.' The compact noun phrase places the battlefield extent before the subject for poetic emphasis.
segments.s088-g16: برفتند is a past, 3rd person plural verb 'they went/advanced' (بر‑ here marks direction/forward motion). با introduces the instrument/means 'with,' and گرزهای گران is a noun + plural suffix + adjective 'heavy maces' (گران after the noun modifies it). The whole phrase means 'they advanced with heavy maces.'
segments.s088-g17: "نبد" is an archaic negative existential meaning "there was not" and here negates the following noun phrase; the sequence "نبد کوه پیدا" literally places the negative verb before the noun+adjective (verb–noun order) and means "no mountain was visible." The preposition "ز" (poetic "from/through") repeats before each material "ز ریگ و ز شخ" to indicate the obstructing substances "through/from sand and from shakh."
segments.s088-g18: The phrase "ز دریا به دریا" is a simple from–to locative: "from sea to sea" ("ز" = from, "به" = to). "کشیدند" is past plural (they) of کشیدن meaning "drew/stretched/pulled," and it governs the object "نخ" (a thread/line). Word order here is prepositional locative + verb + object (they stretched a line across from one sea to the other).
segments.s088-g19: "بیاراست" is a classical past verb meaning "adorned/arranged" (here active past). The verb precedes the subject "قارن" (a proper name) in this poetic inversion. The prepositional phrase "به قلب اندرون" ("into/in the inner heart") uses "به" = into/towards and specifies the place or target of the adorning.
segments.s088-g20: "که" introduces a subordinate clause of purpose/result: "so that/that." "با شاه باشد" uses classical "باشد" (subjunctive/optative third person) meaning "may be/shall be/is to be." The construction "سپه را ستون" uses "را" as the definite object/topic marker and means "(a) pillar for the army" — together: "that he may be the army's pillar with the king."
segments.s088-g21: The locative phrase "چپ شاه" means "the king's left/at the king's left." "گرد" here functions adverbially/locatively (sense "around/near") linking the location to the person named; "تلیمان" is a proper name and "بخاست" is the past tense "rose/stood up." The clause order places the locative phrase before the person and verb, typical in epic diction.
segments.s088-g22: "چو" is a simile marker meaning "like/as." "شاپور نستوه" is the name plus adjective "nestaweh" (distressed/vexed) modifying him. "بر دست راست" is a locative phrase "on the right hand/on the right side," where "بر" = on/upon, "دست" = hand/side, and "راست" = to the right; the whole phrase parallels the preceding clause (Telymān on the left, Shāpūr on the right).
segments.s088-g23: "ز شبگیر تا خور ز گردون بگشت" sets a time span: "from nightfall (ز شبگیر) until (تا) the sun (خور) turned/returned (بگشت) from the firmament/heaven (ز گردون)." Note that "بگشت" is an archaic/poetic past of "گشتن" meaning "turned/returned"; the clause places the temporal phrase before the verb, a common classical ordering.
segments.s088-g24: As in the earlier line, "نبد" is the archaic negative existential "there was not," so "نبد کوه پیدا" means "no mountain was visible." The correlative negation "نه ... نه ..." that follows lists the other missing features: "نه دریا نه دشت" = "neither sea nor plain." Word order places the negator before the noun phrase for emphatic negation.
segments.s088-g25: دل تیغ (literally “heart of the sword”) is a possessive/genitive phrase with the noun دل preceding its modifier تیغ; گفتی here is the past second-person form “you said” (classical word-order places the verb near the phrase it comments on), and ببالد همی is a classical/perfective-present form meaning “would swell / be proud,” with همی marking continuous or habitual aspect. Together the segment reports what was said about the sword (personified).
segments.s088-g26: زمین زیر اسپان is a prepositional phrase ‘the earth under the horses’ (زمین + modifier زیر اسپان). بنالد is the past/imperf. form of نالیدن (to moan/lament) and همی again gives a continuing/habitual sense, so the clause means ‘the earth kept lamenting beneath the horses.’ Word order is typical classical Persian with the locative phrase before the verb.
segments.s088-g27: چو شد is a temporal clause ‘when it happened/when ...’ (چو = when; شد = happened/became). نیزه‌ها is the subject ‘the spears,’ and بر زمین سایه‌دار is the locative phrase ‘on/upon the shaded ground’ (noun + adjective). The clause sets the time/scene for what follows.
segments.s088-g28: شکست is a noun meaning ‘defeat/ruin’ placed before the verb for emphasis; اندر آمد is an idiomatic classical phrase ‘came into/occurred.’ سوی مایه‌دار is a directional/target phrase ‘upon/toward the wealthy (side or camp)’, where مایه‌دار functions substantively (‘the rich’). The whole clause reports that defeat came upon the wealthy party.
segments.s088-g29: چو آمد = ‘when it came/when it happened’; به بخت اندرون تیرگی is a dative/locative expression literally ‘darkness into (his) fortune’s interior,’ i.e. ‘darkness entered his fate/fortune.’ Classical Persian often places the prepositional phrase (به بخت اندرون) before the noun تیرگی it governs.
segments.s088-g30: گرفتند is a past plural verb ‘they seized/took’; ترکان is the agent ‘the Turks’ (here following the verb, a permitted word-order in classical Persian). چیرگی is the noun ‘domination/mastery’ and functions as the thing seized. برو in this line is archaic/unclear in isolation (likely a prepositional/adverbial relic meaning ‘over it’ or a linking particle), so its precise sense is uncertain here but it connects گرفتن to چیرگی.
segments.s088-g31: بران سو is a locative/directional compound ‘on/at that side’ (بران = ‘upon that’ or ‘thereon’; سو = ‘side/toward’). که introduces the explanatory clause ‘that Shapur was ...’, and شاپور نستوه بود is a simple past-stative clause ‘Shapur was distressed’ with نستوه as the predicate adjective and بود as the past linking verb.
segments.s088-g32: پراگنده شد is a passive/inchoative phrase ‘became scattered’ (verb phrase with شد = become). هرک is a universal/indefinite pronoun ‘whoever/whichever,’ and انبوه بود qualifies those people as ‘being numerous/dense’; together the clause means that every host that had been numerous was scattered.
segments.s088-g73: نشستند is a past plural (3rd person) verb meaning 'they sat (down)'; here it functions as the main past action. بر خوان = 'at/on the table' uses the preposition بر + noun (word order: verb then prepositional phrase). و می‌خواستند is a past imperfective ('and they wanted/meant to') linking to the following infinitival/result clause.
segments.s088-g74: زمانی here means 'for a while' (temporal adverb) and modifies the following clause. دل 'heart' is the grammatical object/topic and از غم 'from sorrow' is an ablative phrase showing what the heart is freed of. بپیراستند is a past plural perfective with the prefix بـ (poetic/archaic); here it means 'they rid/cleansed (their) heart(s) of' — an idiomatic, somewhat archaic verb form.
segments.s088-g75: پس آنگه is a doubled sequence adverb ('then/afterwards') for emphasis. سوی + noun gives direction 'toward'; خان قارن is a nominal phrase (title + name) marking the goal/recipient. شدند is past plural 'they became/were' used here as the deictic verb meaning 'they went/turned (toward)' in epic diction.
segments.s088-g76: همه دیده = 'every/ everyone's eye(s)' (subject phrase). چون is a simile marker 'like/as'. ابر بهمن is a poetic image 'the cloud of Bahman' (Bahman as a named/seasonal or poetic reference) and شدند is the past plural copula 'became/turned into' — together: 'all eyes became like the Bahman cloud.'
segments.s088-g77: سخن را uses the object marker را to topicalize/mark 'speech'. فگندند is past plural 'they threw/cast away' (here idiomatically 'they threw away words' = they stopped ordinary talking). هر گونه = 'in every kind/way' (an adverbial phrase) and بن is a short noun 'base/root' used here within the idiom (poetic; it links to the following phrase).
segments.s088-g78: بران is an adverbial/pronominal particle 'thereon/thereupon' linking back to the previous phrase. برنهادند is past plural 'they set forth/raised/exhibited' (compound of بر + نهادن). یکسر is an adverb 'entirely/at once' modifying the action, and سخن repeats 'speech' as the direct object — 'they at once put forward the whole speech.'
segments.s088-g79: که introduces a content clause 'that...'. ما را uses را with the first-person plural as a patient/dative ('us' as the ones affected). سوی پارس = 'toward Pars' (Pars = Persia). باید expresses necessity 'must/it is necessary'. کشید is the verbal stem here ('to draw/move/lead'); in classical epic Persian the past stem is used with باید to express required action: 'we must be led/marched to Pars.'
segments.s088-g80: نباید is the negative modal 'must not/should not'. برین is the fused form of بر + این ('on this / in this [place]') — here 'in this place'. جایگاه 'place/spot' is the noun, and آرمید is past 'rested' (used with the negative modal to mean they should not rest here). The whole phrase: 'we must not rest in this place.'
segments.s088-g57: شبستان ماگر به دست آورد is a conditional clause: ماگر functions here as a conditional particle ('if' / 'unless'), شبستان is the direct object 'inner chamber/bedchamber', and the verbal phrase به دست آورد (literally 'bring to hand') is a fixed verbal expression meaning 'to obtain/seize'. Word order is object + conditional particle + verb phrase, typical in older/poetic Persian.
segments.s088-g58: برین نامداران شکست آورد is the result clause: برین = بر + این ('upon these'), naming the targets; نامداران is a plural adjective/noun 'renowned ones'; شکست آورد is a compound verb 'to bring about defeat' (آورد as past/predicate). Word order: prepositional phrase (upon these) + object + verb.
segments.s088-g59: به ننگ اندرون سر شود ناپدید: این عبارت is a short predication where به ننگ ('with/through shame') gives the cause, اندرون سر 'inside his head/head within' indicates location/subject, and شود ناپدید is a present/future passive-like construction 'becomes vanished' (شود = 'becomes'). The sequence shows cause → location/subject → verb.
segments.s088-g60: به دنب کروخان بباید کشید is an obligation clause: به دنب کَس/چیزی = 'in pursuit of / after X'; کروخان is a proper name; بباید is the modal 'must/ought to' (present form), and کشید is the verb 'to draw/drag/pursue' (inflected here as the action required). Word order: place/pursuit phrase → object (name) → modal → main verb.
segments.s088-g61: ترا خوردنی هست و آب روان: ترا (ت + را) is the object pronoun 'to/for you' (here topical), خوردنی هست is a predication meaning 'there is edible/food' or 'you have food to eat' (هست as existential/copula), and و آب روان links 'and running water' (آب روان = 'running water'). Typical Persian order: topic/object marker → predicate.
segments.s088-g62: سپاهی به مهر تو دارد روان: سپاهی here denotes the armed force/army; به مهر تو = 'out of love/affection for you' (به as 'for/toward', مهر = 'love'); دارد روان uses دارد as an auxiliary meaning 'is (in the process of)' and روان 'moving/flowing' so the phrase means 'an army is moving toward you out of affection'. Word order: subject → circumstantial phrase → auxiliary verb → participle/adjective.
segments.s088-g63: همی باش و دل را مکن هیچ بد: همی باش is an imperative 'remain/continue to be' (همی is an emphatic/adverbial particle), و links the clauses, دل را is the object/topic 'the heart', مکن is the negative imperative 'do not make', هیچ بد qualifies the negation meaning 'in no way/at all bad'. Word order follows imperative + coordinating clause + object + negative imperative + modifier.
segments.s088-g64: که از شهریاران دلیری سزد: که introduces a reason/explanation ('for'/'because'), از شهریاران = 'of/kind to kings' (از = 'from/of', شهریاران = 'kings/lords'), دلیری is the noun 'bravery/valor', and سزد is a stative/impersonal verb 'it befits / it is appropriate'. The clause gives the justification for the previous admonition.
segments.s088-g97: یکی here is an indefinite subject 'someone' (not the numeral). نیزه زد is a light verb construction: نیزه (spear) + زدن (to strike) — 'struck/ thrust with a spear'; زد is past, 3rd singular. بر introduces the target ('at/against/on') — بر کمربند اوی = 'at his belt'. اوی is an archaic/poetic possessive form ('his/its'). Word order places the directional/target phrase after the verb, a typical flexible order in classical Persian poetry.
segments.s088-g98: که introduces a result/explanatory clause here ('so that/and then'), linking the action to its consequence. بگسست is the past (3rd sg.) of گسستن with the archaic verbal prefix ب- (i.e. 'broke apart/was broken'). بنیاد و پیوند اوی lists the affected parts ('its foundation and its fastening/link'), with اوی again the possessive 'its/his'; the verb appears before the nouns (V–O order), a word-order permitted in poetic diction.
segments.s088-g99: سپه is the subject 'the army/host'. سر به سر is an idiom meaning 'one and all' or 'every single one' (literally 'head by head') and functions adverbially. دل‌شکسته is a compound adjective 'broken‑hearted' used predicatively, and شدند is the past plural 'became/were' agreeing with سپه (collective treated with a plural verb).
segments.s088-g100: همه 'all' reinforces the plurality. یک ز دیگر is a poetic distributive phrase 'one from another / one of the others' where ز = 'from' (poetic genitive/ablative). گسسته شدند uses the past participle گسسته ('separated/broken apart') with شدند (past plural) to mean 'were separated' or 'became severed'. The clause states the consequence: they separated from each other.
segments.s088-g101: سپهبد is the subject 'spahbed' (a high military commander). سوی پارس is a directional phrase 'toward Pars' (سوی = toward, پارس = Persia). بنهاد is the past of نهادن with the prefix ب- ('set/laid' → here 'turned/put'), and روی (face) is the object: 'he set/turned his face.' The verb بنهاد appears before its object, a word order common in classical/persuasive styles.
segments.s088-g102: This phrase functions as an appositive/title for the spahbed. ابا here appears to be a proper name or name‑element (not the lexical verb 'to refuse'); that sense is not supplied in the given list. نامور is a postpositive adjective 'renowned' modifying the name, and لشکر جنگ‌جوی is a compound noun 'the warlike army/host' (لشکر = army, جنگ‌جوی = warrior/warriorly) in apposition or as an epithet describing his command.
segments.s088-g65: بدین گروه of words forms an adverbial + subject + verb phrase: کنون (adverb) + من (subject) + شوم (1st‑person subjunctive/optative of شدن). Here شوم expresses the speaker's intention or immediate resolve ('let me / I will become / I shall go'), so the clause reads 'Now I shall set out/in pursuit.' The prepositional cluster بر پی (بر + پی) follows and takes the object این سپاه: بر (toward/on) + پی (in pursuit of) + این سپاه ('this army'). Word order is topical/adverbial first, then subject + finite verb, then the goal phrase.
segments.s088-g66: بگیرم is the 1st‑person present/subjunctive of گرفتن used to state intent ('I will seize/cut off, I will take'). بریشان is a contraction of بر ایشان (literally 'against/upon them') marking the target of the action. ز هر گونه راه is a prepositional phrase (ز = 'from', هر گونه = 'every kind', راه = 'way/path') giving the sense 'from every possible route.' Together: 'I will cut them off from every kind of road.'
segments.s088-g67: بدو گفت نوذر is an inverted dativelocution: بدو ('to him') + گفت (past of 'say', here introducing speech) + نوذر (subject, the speaker). The clause که این رای نیست is a content clause introduced by که ('that') + این رای ('this counsel/plan') + نیست ('is not'), meaning 'that this counsel is not (appropriate/right).' Note the classical/poetic order puts the indirect object first (بدو).
segments.s088-g68: سپه را uses را as a topical/definite marker highlighting 'the army' as the theme. The comparative clause چو تو لشکرآرای نیست reads 'there is no ... like you': چو = 'like/as', تو = 'you', لشکرآرای = 'army‑adorn-er' (i.e., commander/one who arrays the host), نیست = 'is not / there is not.' So the line means 'the army has no commander like you.'
segments.s088-g69: ز بهر is a prepositional phrase meaning 'for the sake of / for the purpose of.' بنه here functions as a noun meaning 'camp/encampment' (poetic usage) or 'the act of pitching camp.' رفت is the past tense 'went.' گستهم و طوس are coordinated proper names (Gostaham and Tous) serving as the subjects who went. The clause order is purpose phrase → verb → coordinated subjects, which is permissible in the epic’s poetic diction.
segments.s088-g70: بدانگه is a temporal adverb meaning 'at that moment/thereupon.' The relative/temporal clause that follows is introduced by که ('when/that'): برخاست is the past of برخاستن ('arose/was raised'), آوای = 'the sound/voice' and کوس = 'war‑drum/signal.' So the whole clause gives the time when Gostaham and Tous went: 'at that moment when the sound of the drum rose.'
segments.s088-g71: بدین زودی is an adverbial phrase meaning 'so soon / immediately'; it combines به + این in poetic contraction. اندر شبستان is a locative phrase ('into/within the chamber/bedchamber') and رسد is a present/factual verb 'arrives/enters' used in narrative to report the action. The sequence gives the quick movement: 'At once he entered the chamber.'
segments.s088-g72: کند here is a 3rd‑person singular present of کردن used to mean 'they/one made/caused' (in epic style a singular verb can follow a plural topic for metrical reasons). ساز ایشان = 'their music/instrument/tune' (saz = the music or instrument). چنان چون سزد is a simile/standard phrase: چنان = 'such', چون = 'as/like', سزد = 'it befits / is fitting.' Together the clause means 'they made/played their music in such a manner as was fitting.'
segments.s088-g33: همی بود شاپور تا کشته شد is a two-part temporal statement: همی بود (همی + بود) gives a continuous/ongoing past sense — 'he was still/continued to be'. تا introduces a temporal subordinate clause 'until', and کشته شد is a passive past construction ('was killed') where شد functions as the auxiliary marking the passive/event.
segments.s088-g34: سر بخت ایرانیان گشته شد: سر بخت is a noun phrase meaning roughly 'the head/chief of fortune' or 'the leading part of fortune' with ایرانیان as its possessor ('of the Iranians'); گشته شد is a compound indicating change/state (گشته 'become/turned' + شد as auxiliary) — 'was turned/was made', i.e. their fortune was overturned or ruined.
segments.s088-g35: از انبوه ترکان پرخاشجوی: از marks origin/'out of/from'; انبوه before the noun qualifies it as 'numerous/a mass of'; ترکان is the head noun 'the Turks'; پرخاشجوی is an adjectival modifier 'quarrelsome/aggressive' modifying ترکان. Persian allows stacked modifiers preceding or following the noun.
segments.s088-g36: به سوی دهستان نهادند روی: به سوی دهستان is a prepositional phrase 'toward the district'; نهادند is a past-plural verb 'they set/placed' (here part of the idiom), and روی ('face') combines with نهادن in the idiom رو نهادن meaning 'to turn one's face/toward' — i.e. 'they turned/advanced toward the district.'
segments.s088-g37: شب و روز بد بر گذرهاش جنگ: شب و روز 'night and day' is adverbial 'constantly'; بد qualifies the following as 'disastrous/unfavorable'; بر introduces location 'upon/on'; گذرهاش is گذر + possessive -ash 'its passes/checkpoints'; جنگ is the noun 'war/battle' — together: 'a fierce fight upon its passes day and night.'
segments.s088-g38: برآمد برین نیز چندی درنگ: برآمد is the verb 'arose/occurred'; برین is a fused form of بر + این ('upon/thereon' or 'thereupon') used adverbially; نیز 'also'; چندی 'for a while'; درنگ 'a pause/delay' — 'thereupon also a brief pause occurred.'
segments.s088-g39: چو نوذر فرو هشت پی در حصار: چو 'when'; نوذر the proper name Nowzar; فرو 'down' (here part of a motion/retreat expression) and پی 'after/following' together produce the sense 'retreat/withdrawal' or 'followed down' into the place; در حصار is the prepositional phrase 'into the fortress' — 'when Nowzar withdrew into the fort.'
segments.s088-g40: برو بسته شد راه جنگ سوار: بسته شد is a passive/resultative 'became closed/was shut' (شد as auxiliary); راه 'road/way'; جنگ سوار is a genitive/attributive sequence 'war-mounted' meaning 'the way of the war-horsemen/cavalry' — the whole clause states that the route for the cavalry to the fighting was closed. برو here functions as a narrative/temporal particle ('thereupon/then') introducing that result.
segments.s088-g49: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when' introducing a subordinate clause. The sequence قارن شنود is subject followed by the archaic third-person present/past-form شنود ('he hears' in older poetic diction used here as 'heard'). آنکه introduces reported content ('that/which'), and the proper name افراسیاب completes that reported clause as its subject/agent.
segments.s088-g50: گسی is a quantifying/indefinite word 'some/a certain' modifying the object phrase لشکر; کرد is the past verb 'did/sent' with لشکر (army) as its object. به هنگام خواب is a temporal prepositional phrase ('at the time of sleep' = while they slept), where به هنگام = 'at the time of'. Note the older word order places the verb after its object phrase.
segments.s088-g51: شد is the past/resultative 'became'. از marks cause ('because of/from') and رشک جوشان is a causal phrase meaning 'boiling/seething envy' (جوشان adjectival). The coordinating و links to دل کرد تنگ, where دل (heart) is object and کرد+تنگ is a causative construction meaning 'made/grew narrow' (i.e. 'his heart was constricted'). This is a typical Persian idiom to express jealous anger or anguish.
segments.s088-g52: بر marks the target of the action ('upon/against Nowzar'), with نوذر the target. آمد is the simple past 'came' used here with بر to mean 'came upon/attacked'. بسان introduces a simile 'like/as', followed by پلنگ 'leopard' describing the manner of his attack.
segments.s088-g53: که here is emphatic/relational 'that' introducing an appositive identification: توران شه 'king of Turan' followed by آن ناجوانمرد مرد, where آن 'that' + ناجوانمرد (an adjective 'dishonourable, cowardly') modifies مرد ('man'). The sequence is appositive/pejorative description of the same person.
segments.s088-g54: نگه کن is the imperative 'look!' formed by the noun نگه ('gaze/watch') plus the auxiliary/imperative کن ('do'), functioning as a command to observe. That is followed by a content clause introduced by که ('that/which') — با شاه ایران marks the person affected ('with/regarding the Shah of Iran') and چه کرد is the interrogative-exclamative 'what he did' (چه = what; کرد = did), asking/pointing out his action.
segments.s088-g55: سوی is the directional preposition 'toward'. روی پوشیدگان is a noun phrase 'the face‑covered (those who have their faces covered)' where پوشیدگان is a participial plural 'those who have covered' functioning as a modifier of روی ('faces' → 'those with covered faces'). سپاه follows as the head noun 'army/host' so the whole phrase means 'toward the army of the veiled-faced' or 'toward the face‑covered troop'.
segments.s088-g56: سپاهی فرستاد is past tense 'he sent a troop/contingent of soldiers' (سپاهی = soldiers/force; فرستاد = he sent). بی + مر ... به راه is an adverbial phrase: بی = 'without' and به راه = 'on the road' (indicating movement). The exact meaning of مر is archaic/uncertain here (see note) so the combined phrase functions as 'sent ... on the road without [something/without delay]'. The verb فرستاد is the main past action linking back to 'he' in prior clauses.
segments.s088-g41: The phrase is a VSO/OV order typical of epic Persian: the direct object 'سواران' (horsemen) appears up front, then the (archaic/poetic) verb 'بیاراست' (perfective, 'arrayed/armed') and finally the agent 'افراسیاب' appears at the end for emphasis. 'بیاراست' is an older compound form meaning 'made ready/arrayed' and is here transitive, taking 'سواران' as its object.
segments.s088-g42: 'گرفتش' is past tense 'گرفت' plus the enclitic object pronoun '-ش' (him/it/them) — a compact way to say 'he took/seized it/them'. Here the verb phrase governs the following prepositional phrase 'ز جنگ' ('from the battle'), while the two nouns 'درنگی' and 'شتاب' are used as abstract nouns to describe manner: 'درنگی' = 'a pause' and 'شتاب' = 'haste'; together they indicate the action was done with haste, i.e. he 'took up haste' (a poetic periphrasis meaning 'he hastened away').
segments.s088-g43: 'یکی' here means 'a certain' (introducing a person), followed by the adjective 'نامور' (renowned) and the noun 'ترک' (Turk). The object marker 'را' marks 'ترک' as definite/specific. 'کرد یاد' is a light-verb construction: 'کرد' (did/made) + noun 'یاد' (remembrance) = 'he remembered' or 'he named/mentioned'. Word order places the modified noun phrase before the verbal phrase.
segments.s088-g44: This is an appositive nominal phrase giving the Turk's rank and name: 'سپهبد' (spahbed, a high military title), 'کروخان' (a compound/name/title), followed by 'ویسه نژاد' — 'ویسه' (the ancestor/name) + 'نژاد' (lineage). The postposed 'نژاد' names the lineage (i.e. 'of the stock of Viseh').
segments.s088-g45: 'سوی پارس' is a prepositional phrase 'toward Pars'. 'فرمود' is past tense, third-person, 'he commanded/said'. The conjunction 'تا' introduces a purpose/jussive clause ('so that / in order that') taking the following verb 'برکشید' in the subjunctive/jussive sense — here 'برکشید' (an older form of 'برکشیدن') means 'raise/lift' and in context idiomatically means 'mount or get ready and set off' (ordered them to set out).
segments.s088-g46: The phrase 'به راه بیابان' = 'onto/along the desert road' ('به' = toward/into, 'راه' = road, 'بیابان' = desert). The short clause 'سر اندر کشید' is idiomatic: 'سر' (head) + 'اندر' (into/within) + 'کشید' (past of 'کشیدن' = 'to draw/pull/put') — here it describes the movement of setting off on the road, literally 'they put (their) heads into' the road, i.e. 'they took the road'. Note: the lemma given for 'کشید' in the word list does not include the motion sense; that verbal sense is the one used here.
segments.s088-g47: 'کزان' = 'from that/whence' and 'سو' = 'side/toward' form a locative phrase 'from that side'. 'بد' ('bad/evil') modifies the action or the force being sent; 'ایرانیان را' is the definite object marked by 'را' ('against the Iranians'), and 'بنه' is used as a concrete noun meaning 'a small band/detachment' (a metaphorical use of the basic sense 'small bundle'), placed at the end as the object of the implied sending — i.e. 'he sent a detachment against the Iranians from that side.'
segments.s088-g48: 'بجوید' is present/past-subjunctive style of 'جستن' meaning 'he seeks/looks for'. The repeated noun 'بنه' (here again 'detachment/band') is the object of 'بجوید'. 'مردم' = 'people' and 'بدتنه' is a compound adjective 'بد‑تنه' meaning 'ill‑bodied / ill‑shapen', qualifying those people. Together: 'the detachment searched for the ill‑bodied people.'
segments.s088-g1: 'ازان پس' is a temporal phrase 'after that / thereupon' (prepositional phrase coming at the clause head). 'بیاسود' is an archaic/perf. form meaning 'was at rest / rested' placed before its subject (verb–subject order is common in classical Persian poetry). 'لشکر' is the subject 'the army' and 'دو روز' is a time adverbial 'two days' that follows the verb.
segments.s088-g2: 'سه دیگر' is a numeral phrase 'three more / three other'. 'چو' introduces a temporal clause here and means 'when'. The verb 'بفروخت' is past tense (3sg) 'parted with / sold', and 'گیتی فروز' is a proper-name phrase (Giti‑Fruz) acting as the subject/object of that verb in the temporal clause; the verb preceding its noun is a normal poetic inversion.
segments.s088-g3: نبد is an archaic negative/existential past ('there was not' / 'did not happen'). The phrase 'شاه را' uses the object/topic marker را to mark the king as the one affected. 'روزگار' functions as the agent/subject ('fate' / 'circumstances') and 'نبرد' is the noun 'battle/struggle' connected into the clause; the word order (verb first, then affected noun, then agent) is idiomatic in epic Persian.
segments.s088-g4: به is the preposition 'into / to'. 'بیچارگی جنگ' is a genitive phrase 'the misery/woefulness of war' (بیچارگی governed by به + noun). The compound predicate 'بایست کرد' is idiomatic: بایست (originally 'stand' or 'must') plus کرد ('to cause / do') yields a passive/causative sense 'was forced / was made to stand' — here meaning 'was forced into / driven into' the wretched war.
segments.s088-g5: ابا here is an archaic verb/noun meaning 'to be reluctant / to refuse' used with the following nominal phrase as its subject; 'لشکر' is 'army/host' and 'نوذر افراسیاب' are proper names (Nowzar, Afrasiab) qualifying that army. The word order places the verb/verb-root before the noun phrase in a compressed poetic line.
segments.s088-g6: چو introduces a simile 'like / as'. 'دریای جوشان' is a compound 'a boiling / seething sea' (noun + participial adjective), 'بد' intensifies negatively ('fierce / violent / ill‑favoured' in poetry), and the conjunction 'و' links a second image 'رود آب' literally 'a river/stream of water' (here 'torrent of water') — a standard pair of violent-water images in Persian poetry.
segments.s088-g7: The verb phrase 'خروشیدن آمد' is a periphrastic construction: the infinitive/noun خروشیدن 'to roar' + آمد 'came' gives 'a roaring came' or 'a roar arose' (common in older/poetic Persian). 'ز' is the preposition 'from', and 'پرده‌سرای' is the place 'curtain‑hall / behind the curtain' (a poetic term for the tent/inner chamber); the whole segment means 'a roar came from the curtain‑hall.'
segments.s088-g8: This is a list of the sounds/phenomena producing the roar. 'ابا' repeats as an agental or emotive word linked to the cry; 'ناله' is 'moan / lament' and 'کوس' is the war‑drum (instrument). The conjunction 'و' links 'هندی درای' — 'هندی' ('Indian') modifies 'درای' (here used adjectivally as 'possessing / wealthy / with Indian pomp') so the phrase evokes lamentation, drum‑beating, and Indian splendor/pomp as the components of the outcry.
segments.s088-g81: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. پوشیده‌رویان (literally 'those with covered/hidden faces') is a poetic compound describing treacherous or secretive people; it precedes the noun it modifies. ایران سپاه follows an older epic word order where the possessor (ایران, 'Iran') precedes the possessed (سپاه, 'host/army'), so the whole phrase means 'the host of Iran'.
segments.s088-g82: اسیران شوند is a passive construction: اسیران ('prisoners') + شوند (3rd pl. of شدن, 'become/are made'), so 'are taken prisoner'. از here introduces the agent in the passive (literally 'by / from'), followed by بد (an adjectival 'evil, bad') modifying کینه‌خواه; کینه‌خواه is a compound meaning 'one who seeks vengeance' (an 'avenger' or 'vengeful person').
segments.s088-g83: که introduces a relative/subordinate clause meaning 'who/that'. گیرد is 3rd‑sg. of گرفتن ('take, seize') used here of taking up a weapon. بدین is the folded form 'به + این' ('to/onto this'), so بدین دشت = 'on/onto this plain'. نیزه به دست is an idiomatic order meaning 'spear in (his) hand' (نیزه = spear, به دست marks 'in the hand').
segments.s088-g84: کرا is an archaic relative pronoun 'who' (older form of که/کسی). باشد is the subjunctive/optative form of بودن here ('may be' / 'be'), giving the wish/nominal quality 'may be at rest'. آرام 'calm/at rest' and جای نشست is a noun phrase 'place of sitting' (a seat); نشست here has the sense 'sitting/seat'. The conjunction و simply links the two predicates.
segments.s088-g85: چو = 'when'. The sequence شیدوش و کشواد و قارن are personal names listed in coordination; بهم 'together' indicates they acted jointly. Note that some of these lexemes can have common meanings (e.g. شیدوش as a noun), but here they function as names in the narrative.
segments.s088-g86: زدند is past‑plural of زدن ('they struck' / 'they did'), used idiomatically with رای to mean 'they put forth / adopted (a) counsel or decision'. اندرین رای = 'in this counsel/decision'. بر بیش و کم = 'regarding more and less' (بر + nouns = 'concerning/on the matter of').
segments.s088-g87: چو ... گذشت is a temporal clause 'when ... had passed'. نیمی ('half') + گذشت ('passed') + از شب ('of the night') gives 'when half the night had passed'. دیریاز is an adverbial/temporal modifier 'after a long time / when it had become late', strengthening the sense that night was well advanced.
segments.s088-g88: دلیران 'the brave ones' is the subject. به رفتن گرفتند ساز is an idiom: گرفتن ساز (literally 'take up the instrument') means 'make preparations' or 'begin the action' — here 'به رفتن' ('for going / to depart') specifies what they prepared for. گرفتند is past plural 'they took up / began', and ساز is the metaphorical 'means / preparation'.
segments.s088-g89: بدین روی is an adverbial idiom ('for this reason, thus'); it functions as a fronted circumstantial. The noun phrase دژدار بد گژدهم follows: دژدار 'castellan' is the head, بد is an attributive adjective 'bad/evil' qualifying the following name گژدهم (a proper name). Word order is typical Persian: adverbial first, then the noun phrase with adjective + name.
segments.s088-g90: دلیران بیدار is a noun + adjective pair ('the valiant, awake ones'); the adjective بیدار follows the noun. با او بهم is a prepositional phrase: با 'with' + او 'him', and بهم here means 'together' (they assembled together with him). The segment is elliptical (no main verb); it describes participants joining the castellan.
segments.s088-g91: وزان (و + زان) is a connective adverbial meaning 'and/from that (there)'; it links this clause to what went before. روی دژ here means 'the side/face of the fortress' (روی used spatially), followed by the proper name بارمان (Barman) and then و سپاه 'and the host/army'—a coordinated noun phrase listing forces.
segments.s088-g92: کوس و پیلان 'drums and elephants' names the instruments/units accompanying the host. نشسته is a predicative participle/ adjective 'seated/positioned' describing their disposition, and به راه ('on the road') is a locative phrase where به here functions as a locative/agentive preposition. The initial token ابا is uncertain/archaic here (not clearly matching the provided senses) and may be a textual form or particle; its meaning is not resolved in the supplied senses.
segments.s088-g93: کزو is a relative/adverbial 'from whom / from which' linking the following description to that person. قارن رزم‌زن is a name + epithet ('Qaran, the warrior') in apposition. خسته بود is a simple past/state: خسته 'weary' + بود 'was' (past auxiliary), describing Qaran's condition.
segments.s088-g94: به خون برادر کمربسته بود is a nominal phrase describing Qaran's state: به خون 'with/by blood' (به used instrumentally/locatively), برادر 'brother' modifies خون, کمربسته 'girt/girded' is a past participle/adjectival predicate, and بود is the past copula 'was' — together: 'he was girded with his brother's blood.'
segments.s088-g95: برآویخت is a past verb 'he leapt up / sprang upon.' چون شیر is a simile 'like a lion' (چون 'like/as' + شیر 'lion'). با بارمان uses با 'with/against' plus the name بارمان; in combat contexts با can indicate direction of the attack (i.e. 'upon/against Barman'). The clause is a vivid single-line action: verb + simile + object.
segments.s088-g96: سوی چاره جستن is an infinitival purpose/attempt phrase: سوی 'toward' + چاره 'a remedy/means' + جستن 'to seek.' ندادش is the past negative of 'give' with the enclitic pronoun -ش 'him' attached (نداد + ش = 'did not give him'). زمان is the abstract subject 'time' (time did not give him the chance). Overall: 'time did not give him the opportunity to look for a solution.'

sentences.s088-s5: A banner rose from the king's court; they set iron helmets upon their heads.
sentences.s088-s6: In Afrasiab's curtained pavilion, no one could fall asleep.
sentences.s088-s7: All night long they arrayed the army and fitted swords and gambesons.
sentences.s088-s8: From plain to peak the armored warriors advanced, with heavy maces.
sentences.s088-s9: No mountain was visible through the sand and shakh; they stretched a line from sea to sea.
sentences.s088-s10: Qāran adorned the inner heart, so that he might be the army's pillar with the king.
sentences.s088-s11: On the king's left Telymān rose up, like Shāpūr the distressed on the right hand.
sentences.s088-s12: From nightfall until the sun turned in the heavens, no mountain was visible — neither sea nor plain.
sentences.s088-s13: You said the sword's heart would swell with pride; the earth beneath the horses kept groaning.
sentences.s088-s14: When the spears struck the shaded earth, ruin befell the wealthy side.
sentences.s088-s15: When darkness entered his fortune, the Turks seized control and gained the upper hand.
sentences.s088-s16: On that side—for Shapur was distraught—every dense host was scattered.
sentences.s088-s37: They sat down at the table and wished, for a while, to rid their hearts of sorrow.
sentences.s088-s38: Then they went to Khan Qaran; all eyes were like the cloud of Bahman.
sentences.s088-s39: They cast aside speech; in every manner they put it forward — at once the whole speech.
sentences.s088-s40: that we must be taken toward Pars; we must not rest in this place.
sentences.s088-s29: If he should seize the inner chamber, he would bring defeat upon those renowned men.
sentences.s088-s30: He will vanish inside in shame; he must be dragged in pursuit of Kurokhan.
sentences.s088-s31: You have food to eat and running water; an army, moved by love for you, is on the march.
sentences.s088-s32: Remain steadfast and do not make your heart at all ill, for bravery befits kings.
sentences.s088-s49: Someone struck at his belt with a spear, so that its foundation and fastening were broken.
sentences.s088-s50: The army, one and all, became broken‑hearted; all were separated from one another.
sentences.s088-s51: The spahbed Aba Namvar, the renowned commander of the warlike host, turned his face toward Pars.
sentences.s088-s33: Now I will go in pursuit of this army and cut them off from every kind of road.
sentences.s088-s34: Nowzar said to him that this counsel is not right; the army has no army‑commander like you.
sentences.s088-s35: For the purpose of encamping, Gostaham and Tous went to that place when the sound of the drum rose.
sentences.s088-s36: Immediately he entered the bedchamber, and they made music in such a way as was fitting.
sentences.s088-s17: Shapur remained until he was killed; the fortunes of the Iranians were overturned.
sentences.s088-s18: From the mass of quarrelsome Turks they turned their faces toward the district.
sentences.s088-s19: A hard battle arose day and night on its passes; moreover, there was for a while a pause there.
sentences.s088-s20: When Nowzar withdrew into the fortress, the road for the cavalry to the battle was closed.
sentences.s088-s25: When Qaren heard that Afrasiab had sent some troops during sleep.
sentences.s088-s26: He became seething with jealousy and his heart grew tight; he sprang at Nowzar like a leopard.
sentences.s088-s27: See what that Turanian king — that dishonourable man — did to the Shah of Iran.
sentences.s088-s28: Toward the face‑covered troop he sent a contingent of soldiers and dispatched them onto the road without delay.
sentences.s088-s21: Afrasiab arrayed the horsemen and, without delay, hastened away from the battle.
sentences.s088-s22: He called to mind a famed Turk: the spahbed Kro-khan, of Viseh's lineage.
sentences.s088-s23: He ordered them toward Pars to mount and set out; they took the desert road.
sentences.s088-s24: From that side he sent a detachment against the Iranians; the detachment sought out the ill‑bodied people.
sentences.s088-s1: After that the army rested two days and three more, when Giti‑Fruz parted with the world.
sentences.s088-s2: Fate did not spare the king; it forced him into the misery of war.
sentences.s088-s3: Nowzar and Afrasiab's host surged and refused like a seething sea and a rushing torrent of water.
sentences.s088-s4: A roar rose from the curtain-hall: Aba's lament, the beating of the drum, and Indian splendour.
sentences.s088-s41: When the covert‑faced ones of Iran's host are taken captive by an evil avenger.
sentences.s088-s42: so that whoever takes up a spear on this plain may be at rest and have a place to sit.
sentences.s088-s43: When Shidūsh and Keshvād and Qāran together adopted this counsel concerning more and less.
sentences.s088-s44: When half the night had passed and it had grown late, the brave men began to make ready to depart.
sentences.s088-s45: For this reason the castellan Bad‑Gozdeham — and the brave, awakened warriors — gathered with him.
sentences.s088-s46: And from that side (of the fortress) Barman and the host — with drums and elephants — were stationed on the road.
sentences.s088-s47: From him Qaran the warrior was weary; he was girded with his brother's blood.
sentences.s088-s48: He sprang up like a lion against Barman; time did not give him the chance to seek a remedy.
